data.title: Manole Capital: Q2'25 Investor Newsletter
data.description:
Manole Capital Management

2nd Quarter 2025 Investor Newsletter

July 2025

Macro Thoughts:

Despite its challenges, the US economy remains the envy of the world. It is driven by resilience, innovation, and ingenuity, and we do not see anything that changes that opinion. While concerns linger, recent data points to a strong rebound in the 2nd quarter of 2025 GDP to +2.4%, a notable improvement from last quarter’s contraction. Earlier this year, we expected a healthy market backdrop, along with a resurgence in M&A and IPO activity, but the tariff situation (called “Liberation Day”) seemed to pause that momentum. In April, we wrote 5 pages on the market uncertainty stemming from President Trump’s tariff announcement, so it isn’t necessary to rehash those thoughts again.

The only certainty is uncertainty, and we expect volatility to persist for the foreseeable future. This type of environment tends to be our “sweet spot,” where we can generate alpha and outperform. This might be aided by our large position in the exchanges, or because we remain disciplined in our focus on free cash flow and durable business models. Regardless of the environment, we are expecting continued volatility.

Inflation:

Inflation remains a concern, but it is trending in the right direction. Is it at the Fed’s 2% target? No, but the last mile of a marathon is always the hardest (so we’ve been told). Geopolitical risks are certainly elevated right now, with multiple conflicts across the globe. Russia and Ukraine are still at war, and Israel has undertaken a massive initiative to remove Iran’s nuclear capabilities. These geopolitical conflicts are likely to keep oil and energy markets volatile and difficult to forecast. Last year, the Fed was projecting core inflation (their preferred measure) would be 2.5% this year, falling to 2.2% in 2026, and reaching their 2.0% target by 2027. However, this year, core inflation is running at 3.1%, 60 basis points higher. Has the FOMC median number of rate cuts changed? Not yet.

At his FOMC announcement, Fed Chairman Powell said, "Despite elevated uncertainty, the economy is in a solid position, the unemployment rate remains low, and the labor market is at or near maximum employment." Does this sound like somebody who is in a hurry to cut rates? Chairman Powell, the Fed, and central bankers are typically a very conservative group. With a dual mandate of maximum employment and stable prices (i.e., low inflation), the Fed will always choose to be prudent and rely on the data.

On June 18th, the Fed kept rates unchanged at 4.25% to 4.50% for the fourth consecutive meeting, with their last cut coming in December. It looks as if the Fed is OK to let core inflation run a bit higher than its 2% target for the near future, without lowering rates. Rather than guessing or playing economist, we rely on market expectations, best captured on the CME’s FedTool. As you can see, the market is still expecting two rate cuts by the end of this year, and we anticipate the Fed to remain data-dependent and conservative. Despite President Trump’s constant insults of Fed Chairman Powell, we envision the FOMC will choose to remain patient. The clear message has been that no immediate policy adjustments are warranted, and a wait-and-see approach remains appropriate amid ongoing uncertainty.

Despite the macro uncertainties, we remain focused on the FINTECH sector and true to our disciplined investment process. We anticipate that the Fed will lower interest rates, preserving some of its policy tools for future economic challenges. Rather than speculating on rate changes, our priority remains delivering value and generating performance for our clients.

Our philosophy is unchanged: focus on long-term growth, free cash flow, and staying prepared for the unexpected—regardless of short-term volatility. We will not chase rallies or deviate from our long-term investment strategy. History has shown that markets thrive despite a “wall of worry,” and can often use this uncertainty as fuel for growth. We believe this is the best path forward.

Conclusion:

We are halfway through the year, and the market has seen a nearly 20% decline followed by a remarkably swift rebound. While equity investors have only posted modest gains year-to-date, optimism is gradually returning. Tensions in the Middle East appear to be easing, and trade uncertainty is giving way to more formalized agreements. Inflation has moderated and now sits just above the Fed’s target, while the US economy continues to demonstrate resilience with healthy economic data. Americans remain gainfully employed and continue to spend, and companies have adapted impressively, reporting better-than-expected earnings. We remain optimistic about our ability to generate alpha on your behalf and believe market conditions are favorable for potential outperformance.

Earlier this year, Manole Capital celebrated its 10th anniversary — a milestone we couldn’t have achieved without your trust and loyalty. We remain dedicated to our FINTECH-focused vision, providing access to compelling opportunities in this dynamic and fast-evolving sector.

As always, please don’t hesitate to reach out. We value our relationship and are always here to connect.

Warren Fisher, CFA

Founder & CEO

Manole Capital Management

warren@manolecapital.com

data.title: Semiconductors Winners And Losers At The Start Of H2 2025
data.description:
The second quarter of 2025 was certainly a volatile one for semiconductor stocks. The quarter started with a major selloff after the tariff dispute boiled over, only to be followed by an ever bigger rally. As a result, semis head into H2 2025 with momentum, even if some are not doing as well as others. However, semis may encounter resistance on the way higher, especially since the headwinds that caused problems earlier in the year have not really gone away. Why will be covered next.

Semis are on a roll, but resistance could be standing in the way

A previous article at the start of Q2 2025 was written after the conclusion of a rather weak Q1 2025 for semis. The first quarter got an early scare with the emergence of DeepSeek, which was then followed by trade tensions towards the latter part of the quarter. The U.S. government had signaled the imminent arrival of tariffs in the early part of Q2 2025, which is why the article reasoned there was likely more downside ahead for semis.

The stock market did not react well to “Liberation Day” in early April with a major selloff, and semis were not left unscathed. For instance, the PHLX Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), which tracks 30 companies in the industry, fell to a new 52-week low of $148.31 in early April. However, SOXX has recovered all losses with a huge turnaround, and it is now up 10.8% YTD, as shown in the chart above.

What’s worth noting here is that the rebound in SOXX occurred in the vicinity of a major support level. The chart shows how SOXX rallied from a low of $95.94 to a high of $267.24, and the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of the rally from $95.94 to $267.24 is $161.38. The significance of this is that while SOXX did at one point trade below $161.38, SOXX only managed to close below $161.38 for just one day, or April 8, to be more specific, when the stock closed at $154.86.

This suggests support held, which paved the way for the ensuing rally because SOXX did not break it by staying long enough below support, before it proceeded to turn higher. However, while support held its ground, which allowed for SOXX to follow a trendline on the way higher, it appears there is an obstacle ahead. SOXX has struggled in the last few days to move higher after closing at $238.70 on June 30, which is interesting because it is close to a price region where SOXX has struggled before.

A close-up of the price action of the past 12 months, as shown in the chart above, reveals how SOXX has tended to stall whenever it came close to $240 or so. This happened several times, including last August, September, October, and January. SOXX reached the $238-242 region a few days ago, and it appears it is once again stalling out and having difficulty getting over the hump.

This repeated stalling in roughly the same price region points to the presence of resistance close by, which SOXX will need to overcome to move higher. But the last several times SOXX got to where it is right now, the stock proceeded to turn lower. This does not necessarily have to happen again, and it is possible for SOXX to break through resistance, but this is, at the very least, something worth keeping an eye on with Q3 2025 upon us.

Who was hot and who was not

The first couple of weeks were no good, but in general, Q2 2025 was a very good quarter for semis. SOXX, for instance, gained 26.9% in Q2 2025, including 16.5% in June alone, which allowed SOXX to erase the losses from Q1 2025 to end H1 2025 up 10.8% for the year. In doing so, SOXX outperformed both the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), which gained 5.4% and 7.9%, respectively, YTD. SOXX still underperformed in the three quarters preceding Q2 2025, which is why SOXX is still down 3.2% TTM, much worse compared to the double-digit gains for SPY and QQQ during the same period.

Still, not all semis fared as well as SOXX did. Some did much better, and some lagged behind. The 30 stocks in SOXX are Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Texas Instruments (TXN), Qualcomm (QCOM), Micron (MU), Marvell (MRVL), Microchip (MCHP), Lam Research (LRCX), KLA Corp (KLAC), Applied Materials (AMAT), Intel (INTC), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR), Analog Devices (ADI), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company or TSMC (TSM), ASML (ASML), On Semiconductor/Onsemi (ON), Teradyne (TER), Entegris (ENTG), Skyworks (SWKS), Qorvo (QRVO), STMicroelectronics (STM), Universal Display Corp (OLED), Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC), MKS Instruments (MKSI), ASE Technology (ASX), Arm Holdings (ARM), United Microelectronics Corporation (UMC) and Onto Innovation (ONTO). Of the 30 stocks present in SOXX, 26 ended Q2 2025 with gains, led by AVGO with a gain of 64.6%, ARM with a gain of 51.5% and NVDA with a gain of 45.8%. YTD, at the midpoint of 2025 after two quarters, the top gainers are MU with a gain of 46.5%, KLAC with a gain of 42.2% and ARM with a gain of 31.1%. TTM, the winners are AVGO with a gain of 71.7%, TSM with a gain of 30.3% and NVDA with a gain of 27.9% after 12 months.

The table shows how semis have gotten significantly stronger lately. Only 9 out of 30 stocks in SOXX are up TTM, but this number changes to 22 out of 30 in the past two quarters, 26 out of 30 in the last quarter, and all 30 stocks in SOXX ended June with gains. The group with the most momentum heading into H2 2025 includes MU, ARM, MRVL, and AMD.

At the other end of the spectrum are the losers, and they include ONTO with a loss of 16.8% in Q2 2025, ENTG with a loss of 7.8% and LSCC with a loss of 6.6%. YTD, the list is headed by ONTO with a loss of 39.4%, MRVL with a loss of 29.9% and TER with a loss of 28.6%. TTM, the rankings are ONTO with a loss of 54%, ENTG with a loss of 40.4% and TER with a loss of 39.4%.

Can semis keep rallying higher?

The numbers posted show how semis have gone from underperforming to outperforming as they head into Q3 2025. This change can be attributed to the headwinds losing strength, which allowed for focus to shift towards the tailwinds for semis. If H2 2025 is to turn out more like the latter part of H1 2025 for semis, when they rallied higher, and not like the earlier part of H1 2025, when they sold off, this will need to continue.

Semis were hit by a couple of headwinds in 2025, but they were able to overcome the initial drop to rally higher. The emergence of DeepSeek in early 2025, for instance, brought to the forefront the issue of how companies will recoup the heavy investments in AI, especially if they are confronted with competing options that offer a similar service at a lower cost. This issue remains an open one, but the market took solace from the fact that the boom in AI continues unabated by being a recipient of heavy investments, which is reflected in the strong results reported by companies like NVDA or, more recently, MU.

Another major issue that caused semis to wobble was the decision of the U.S. government to impose tariffs on most countries, which led to a major stock market selloff in early April, but the market liked the fact that related parties seem to be working on finding a resolution to the dispute, even though the issue has not been fully resolved as of yet. This allowed for semis to take off after the drop in early April.

The easing of these headwinds, which led to a drop in semis, enabled the recovery in semis. Furthermore, not only did semis benefit from the easing up of headwinds, but they were further assisted when attention shifted towards potential tailwinds, specifically the prospect of imminent stimulus.

Not only is the U.S. government pursuing an expansionary policy through fiscal stimulus in the form of a tax and spending bill, but the administration has called on the Federal Reserve to supplement the fiscal stimulus with monetary stimulus in the form of lower interest rates. The former is almost certain to pass, but the latter is less certain.

The latest dot plots released by the Fed after the June meeting point to two rate cuts in 2025 as most likely, which would lower the Fed Funds Rate or FFR from the current 4.25-4.50% to 3.75-4.00%. However, almost as many Fed participants were in favor of keeping the current FFR unchanged for the rest of the year.

Nevertheless, the stock market has rallied because it expects monetary easing to come with, for instance, futures seeing a FFR of 3.50-3.75%, or three rate cuts in H2 2025, as the most likely endpoint in 2025. Another five cuts in 2026 are assigned the highest probability, which would lower the FFR to 2.25-2.50%. If the futures market is correct, the stock market can look forward to a total of eight rate cuts, or 200 basis points, in the next 18 months. Stocks, semis in particular, tend to do well when there is stimulus, and there is a lot of stimulus expected in the near future.

Could the rally in semis get derailed?

What happens to semis in H2 2025 and whether they rally higher or get derailed will depend to a large extent on what the U.S. government does in the coming months. The U.S. government played a large role in shaping how H1 2025 turned out for semis, and this could happen again in H2 2025. The selloff in March/April was the direct consequence of actions taken by the U.S. government, and the same can be said of the rally that followed, which was triggered by the U.S. government softening its stance, particularly after bond yields rose in the week following “Liberation Day”.

The U.S. government has been more supportive of stocks lately with policies that are seen to benefit stocks, such as tax cuts, but it has also shown the ability to pursue policies that are a headwind to semis, including the use of tariffs, which can be disruptive to trade in general. The U.S. Administration has eased earlier stock market concerns about trade disputes with the promise of imminent trade deals, but it remains to be seen whether trade partners, China in particular, will be willing to agree to U.S. trade demands.

How the U.S. government reacts to, for instance, a stalemate in trade talks if or when they appear can affect how semis do in H2 2025. If the U.S. decides to force a deal through the use of higher tariffs, something the administration has left open as an option, then a renewed selloff in semis is not out of the question.

Is the Fed in a tough spot?

If there is one wild card that has the potential to sway how H2 2025 turns out for semis, it is likely to be Fed policy. The stock market has priced in a large number of rate cuts in the coming quarters, but the ability of the Fed to deliver on those rate cuts is fraught with risk. If the Fed does not cut, which almost as many governors felt was warranted as those that favored rate cuts, then less easing, which has not been priced in, will need to be accounted for by stocks.

If the Fed gives in to what the U.S. Administration and the stock market itself are clamoring for, which is to ease monetary policy by cutting rates, the Fed risks putting itself in a position it may be forced to do a 180, which would damage its credibility. The proposed spending bill in the U.S. Congress is likely to boost growth, but it is also likely to increase inflationary pressure, especially since the bill is very likely to lead to increased deficit spending.

The impact of higher tariffs has yet to filter through into the economy, but it is also likely to increase inflationary pressure in the near future. The U.S. dollar has been weakening, and this is likely to continue if the Fed goes on the rate cut spree expected from it, which will further boost inflationary pressure. If inflation goes up, which looks probable since there are so many potential drivers out there, the Fed could be forced to undo any rate cuts. Stocks are unlikely to react well to this outcome.

Investor takeaways

Semis put in a strong Q2 2025 after a weak Q1 2025, which erased earlier losses to push YTD gains into positive territory at over 10%. Semis can still count on being the beneficiary of the boom in AI, which is reflected in the list of top performers like AVGO, NVDA, and MU, all of whom can credit their position to being key enablers of AI, whether it is in high-speed networks, GPUs for training or inference and high-bandwidth memory.

Yet, the main factor that determined how Q2 2025 played out was not AI, but the U.S. government, or more specifically, the policies it chose to pursue. It was the U.S. government that was responsible for creating the stock selloff in early April, but it was also the one that made the major rally that followed a reality. The former was caused by the tariff policy pursued by the U.S. government, and the latter was made possible when the U.S. government eased up on its stance towards tariffs. The U.S. government has also arguably placed the Fed in a dilemma in terms of how to set monetary policy since many of the policies being pursued are likely to be inflationary in practice, something the Fed is obligated to keep under control.

The role played by these policies overshadowed all else. SOXX, for instance, gained a strong 27% in Q2 2025 after losing 13% in Q1 2025, even though the two quarters were not that different in terms of the overall state of the semiconductor industry, or overall demand for semiconductor chips, for that matter. For these two quarters to turn out so radically different shows how much of an impact government policies can have on stocks.

This is likely to continue in the short term. How H2 2025 will play out is likely to depend to a large extent on whether the U.S. government chooses to enact policies that favor semis, like in the latter part of H1 2025 or, alternatively, whether it opts to go with policies that go against semis, like in the early part of H1 2025. At the midpoint of the year, with Q3 2025 upon us, the U.S. government is mostly a tailwind, but it is not impossible for it to regress and turn into a headwind.

Semis could go either way in H2 2025, depending on which U.S. government shows up. It could be the one from Q1 2025 or the one from Q2 2025. This makes it rather risky to bet heavily in either direction, long or short, because either side could have the tables turn on it, depending on which policies come into force.

In this environment where uncertainty is high, and the direction of stocks can change on a dime in an instant, it is prudent to hedge accordingly. Put options have a role to play here, and they have become cheaper with the easing of volatility. They provide a measure of protection that could become useful in H2 2025, especially if trade tensions rise again. Another possibility is to go with long/short pairs. For instance, AMD/INTC, LRCX/AMAT, or ADI/TXN are just some of the potential pairs that can be used, regardless of whether semis go up or down.

What would not be prudent is to bet exclusively on any particular direction. Both longs and shorts can have the rug pulled from under them if, in the case of the former, there are signs inflation is picking up when the full impact of higher tariffs is passed on to the consumer, or, in the case of the latter, if the Fed relents by giving in to calls to add to the stimulus being unleashed on stocks.

In the end, semis head into H2 2025 on a roll with momentum on their side, but whether they can maintain this momentum is likely to depend to a great extent on whether they get a U.S. government that is more like the one in Q2 2025 or the one from Q1 2025. At this time, semis are doing well in an environment that resembles the former more than the latter, but be prepared if this changes because the U.S. government decides to change tack and reverts back to being more like how it was during most of Q1 2025.

data.title: XLV: The S&P 500's Most Discounted Sector? Uncover The Hidden Risk
data.description:
The healthcare segment is the most earning-driven sector currently trading at a relative valuation discount within the S&P 500, and I believe that The Health Care Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLV) represents this discrepancy well. Putting aside “political” expectations for a moment, the combination of declining prices in some pharmaceutical giants and stable, if not growing, earnings has made the forward P/E relatively attractive compared to its historical average and to the S&P 500. This is a fact. What we can discuss, however, is whether it’s a positive discrepancy or a trap; in other words, the reasons why the gap has formed. And in detail, some concerning…

Critical Issues of XLV

“Poorly diversified, thus too concentrated on a few stocks, with ‘value only’ characteristics and overly exposed to political decisions.” These are the main criticisms directed primarily at XLV, and more generally at the healthcare segment. Reasons that would theoretically reduce the value created by the Gap are measurable using the multiples method. My mission here is to understand whether these criticisms are supported by data or are rather just common clichés. And I’ll tell you right away that my opinion on XLV, and on the healthcare segment, remains quite optimistic.

I'll start with the technical considerations:

  • 63 holdings (100% U.S.-based), mostly large caps, with a turnover of 2% do justify the criticism: not very diversified. Personally, I believe this actually gives XLV the gift of “representativeness,” which is a quality that has somewhat been lost with the trend of “over-diversification.” Not to mention a certain tax efficiency.

  • I think the fact that the top 5 holdings account for over 37% of the ETF is a consequence of the U.S. market structure, not XLV’s fault. On the contrary, the fact that XLV excludes countless small caps, many from the biotech segment, gives me a sense of “cleanliness” in its selection. With small caps included, the average P/E ratio of the sector would exceed 30x, as is the case, for example, with competitor VHT. Personally, I really like knowing what I’m buying. Clearly, I like knowing which companies I have in my portfolio. Then again, if I decided to get exposure to the emerging biotech segment, I’d probably look for a different ETF.

However, I acknowledge that this is a personal opinion and that in certain portfolios, even VHT has a reason to exist.

“Yeah, XLV is value-oriented indeed”

In my view, the “value only” characteristic of healthcare is a historical legacy. And when it comes to XLV, this is a criticism I often hear, precisely because it lacks small caps, which are theoretically expected to play a central role in the CAGR growth of ETFs. It’s also a belief deeply rooted in wealth management networks: I work in the wealth management segment as a portfolio analyst, and I’ve noticed that many advisors tend to treat XLV as if it were an ETF on consumer staples or any other defensive sector.

But let me ask you a question: is a sector with expected earnings growth for 2025, and even for CY 2026, above 11% a characteristic of a value-oriented sector? Year after year, healthcare ranks among the top 3 in EPS CAGR growth within the S&P 500, alongside tech and communication services. Technically, this doesn’t exclude a value nature, but personally, these numbers sound a lot more like growth stocks to me. In fact, they’re exactly what GARP investors are looking for.

Now we can play with the numbers

Since the market started pricing in political expectations rather than expected earnings, a window has opened. And now I’d like to take a look through it: The forward P/E of XLV is around 16–17x, a level that places it only at the 39th percentile of the 10-year distribution of U.S. healthcare P/Es. This actually makes it the only earning-driven sector of the S&P 500 currently trading below the 50th percentile. (And by earning-driven, I mean sectors where price movements have been guided by earnings expectations, which is a key difference compared to sectors that are consistently discounted, like energy or real estate, for example).

The reason?

Let’s start with the relative one: firstly, thanks to forward EPS growth (+14.3% in 2025 according to FactSet) that’s well above the historical average of the S&P 500 (9.2%). Then the actual, technical reason: the price dropped due to political uncertainty.

And since I’m unable to quantify political risk, and even more so, since it’s hard to predict how the market will price it going forward, I’m switching the field of analysis and moving toward technical analysis, fully aware of one thing: there might be alpha here, in relative terms.

Valuation and Risk

Personally, I find an answer in technical analysis, even though I usually prefer not to rely on it. The bottom price identified in the last update was $132, a level that has now been reached. So at this point, I’m simply waiting for trend confirmation.

In this sense, I believe that a weekly close above $138 could be a technical signal not to ignore; it would break above the 200-period moving average on the 1W timeframe, and for an “active” investor, that would provide a clear chart reference to distinguish a “trap” from a “new trend.”

Risk

We’re finally addressing the real risk: politics. Have you noticed that this time around, with President Donald Trump, the market chose not to stage one of its classic V-shaped recoveries? The same can’t be said for the S&P 500. XLV seems to be consolidating right near its lows.

Clearly, in this case, political impact would directly affect the entire supply chain, and even if not all analysts agree, likely the margins of pharma companies as well.

In short, while political risk is by nature unpredictable, it’s also true that it could have very real consequences on the financials of a segment like healthcare that leans heavily toward pharma. That’s the reason why the S&P 500 keeps climbing, and healthcare doesn’t, despite having superior EPS CAGR.

Conclusion

Since I can’t anticipate the political consequences, and considering that, without this risk, a clear gap has opened between fundamentals and price on XLV, I believe the best solution is to proceed through “attempts” using technical analysis.

That’s why I feel reasonably confident in upgrading my rating from Hold to Buy.

data.title: 4 Upcoming Dividend Increases
data.description:
Depending on the timing of the article's release, the markets will probably be closed for the July 4th holidays in the US. The good news is that all of the ex-dividend dates for the upcoming companies start on Tuesday. That means, if you're interested, you can buy on Monday and get the dividend.

Companies that consistently increase their dividends are often seen as excellent long-term investments, reflecting their strong financial health and stability. Regular dividend increases indicate that a company is generating substantial cash flow, offering both income and total returns for investors to enjoy!

I track dividend increases from strong companies along with other important data points. I've compiled this information as part of my investment strategy, and I’m thrilled to share my insights with my Seeking Alpha community. These lists are crafted to help you in making informed investment decisions.

This week, I'll highlight more companies and present additional information about them, as well as the methodology behind the selection.

Here are some highlights about the group this week:

  • The average increase is 3.1% with a median of 2.2%.
  • The average streak length is 12.5 years, with a median of 9.5 years.
  • All four companies have a significantly higher yield than the broader S&P.

My investment approach centers around buying, holding, and adding to positions in companies that both pay increasing dividends and can also provide better-than-average returns (determined by beating a relevant benchmark).

How I Created The Lists

The information presented here is the result of combining two data sources: the “U.S. Dividend Champions” spreadsheet and upcoming dividend data from NASDAQ. This combination meshes data on companies with a track record of consistent dividend growth and the timeliness of their dividend increases. It's important to note that every company in this list has at least 5 years of dividend growth history.

To be considered for this list, a company must offer higher total annual dividends. Although a company might not increase its dividend every calendar year, its overall annual dividend can still grow.

What Is The Ex-Dividend Date?

The ex-dividend date is when you must own shares to qualify for an upcoming dividend or distribution. To be eligible, you must have bought the shares by the end of the preceding business day. For instance, if the ex-dividend date is Tuesday, you must have acquired the shares by the market close on Monday. If the ex-dividend date falls on a Monday (or a Tuesday following a holiday on Monday), you must have purchased the shares by the previous Friday.

Dividend Streak Categories

Here are the definitions of the streak categories, as I'll use them throughout the piece.

  • King: 50+ years.
  • Champion/Aristocrat: 25+ years.
  • Contender: 10–24 years.
  • Challenger: 5+ years.
Category Count
King 0
Champion 1
Contender 1
Challenger 2

The Dividend Increases List

The data is sorted by the ex-dividend date (ascending) and then by the streak (descending):

Name Ticker Streak Forward Yield Ex-Div Date Increase Percent Streak Category
John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (WLY) 26 3.32 8-Jul-25 0.71% Champion
First Bancorp, Inc (ME) (FNLC) 13 5.6 8-Jul-25 2.78% Contender
General Mills, Inc. (GIS) 6 4.53 10-Jul-25 1.67% Challenger
Darden Restaurants, Inc. (DRI) 5 2.74 10-Jul-25 7.14% Challenger

Field Definitions

Streak: Years of dividend growth history are sourced from the U.S. Dividend Champions spreadsheet.

Forward Yield: The payout rate is calculated by dividing the new payout rate by the current share price.

Ex-Dividend Date: This is the date you need to own the stock.

Increase Percent: The percent increase.

Streak Category: This is the company's overall dividend history classification.

Show Me The Money

Here is a table that shows the new and old rates and the percentage increase. The table is sorted by ex-dividend day in ascending order and dividend streak in descending order.

Ticker Old Rate New Rate Increase Percent
WLY 0.35 0.36 0.71%
FNLC 0.36 0.37 2.78%
GIS 0.6 0.61 1.67%
DRI 1.4 1.5 7.14%

Additional Metrics

Some different metrics related to these companies include yearly pricing action and the P/E ratio. The table is sorted the same way as the table above.

Ticker Current Price 52 Week Low 52 Week High PE Ratio % Off Low % Off High
WLY 43.4 36.5 53.96 10.85 19% Off Low 20% Off High
FNLC 26.44 22.11 31.05 10.24 20% Off Low 15% Off High
GIS 53.83 50.19 75.9 15.99 7% Off Low 29% Off High
DRI 219.33 135.87 228.27 20.51 61% Off Low 4% Off High

Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the “Chowder Rule,” the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
FNLC 5.6 2.9 4 3.8 5.6 9.4
GIS 4.53 3.5 5.4 4.1 3.8 8.6
WLY 3.32 0.7 0.7 -3.6 4.9 -0.3
DRI 2.74 7.2 9.7 10.2 9.6 12.8

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market and grow their dividend payouts over time. For broad exposure to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector, I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

SCHD boasts a strong performance record, offers a yield that exceeds that of the S&P 500, and consistently delivers increasing dividends. With over $68B in assets, it's an incredibly popular dividend growth ETF. The ten-year dividend growth rate is one of the four main factors in the index that SCHD tracks.

Based on this analysis (and further due diligence), I have included several companies in my portfolio and will sometimes make timely purchases of existing holdings. The charts below assume dividends are reinvested.

As our reference point, SCHD has returned 196% over the past decade. Now, from there, we have an extremely wide set of returns from these companies. The only great performer was Darden Restaurants. The owner of Olive Garden, LongHorn Steakhouse, Ruth's Chris, and more returned 365% over the past decade. Interestingly, their performance frequently meandered above and below SCHD's performance. It's really only been in the past 15 months or so that they have separated significantly from SCHD.

Unfortunately, from there, the results just get a lot worse. FNLC was the next "best" performer behind SCHD with a 116% total return. Prior to 2020, it actually demonstrated a level of outperformance over the benchmark. That changed after COVID and the regional banking crisis, and shares have been relatively stagnant since.

GIS and WLY, I don't have a lot of positive things to say about. These two clearly should not be individual holdings in a portfolio at this time; there are plenty of better options out there for dividend growth. I would prefer to hold SCHD in this case. Both are serial laggards, and I see no reason for that to change anytime soon.

Next Steps

I tend to be quite selective about the individual holdings in my portfolio. I'm on the lookout for companies that not only provide significant alpha over SCHD, but really stand out from it. Furthermore, I believe in the importance of consistent, strong earnings and dividend growth over time. These articles provide me (and I hope you) a chance to review what’s performed well and to take a closer look at my current holdings.

I'm not actually interested in looking at any of these companies any closer. DRI is the closest one, but I think there's better restaurant concepts holding companies out there that also sport a better track record as well. In any event, feel free to dive in closer on these names.

I’d love to hear your thoughts on my strategy, so feel free to share yours in the comments below! As always, do your due diligence before making any investment decisions.

data.title: Intel Value Trap: The Potential 18A To 14A Shift In Foundry
data.description:
In our last Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) coverage, we noted our increasing wariness over Intel’s heightening revenue exposure in China despite intensifying geopolitical and regulatory headwinds. Our earlier concerns that the U.S. semiconductor export curbs, which kicked off in late 2022, could risk spilling over into PC chips currently dominating Intel’s sales are starting to materialize. The U.S. government has continued to prioritize export restrictions on advanced AI processors and related semiconductor technologies to China for now, and has yet to put a direct target on advanced PC chips supplied by Intel. But adjacent regulations, such as Taiwan’s recent addition of Huawei – a key Intel customer – to its version of the Entity List, are amplifying risks to Intel’s critical China market sales pipeline.

Meanwhile, the upcoming July 9 deadline on tariff decisions are also poised to levy a structural shift in Intel’s cost structure to the downside. Not only are semiconductors exposed to sectoral tariffs currently estimated at about 25%, but Intel’s significant China revenue mix also subjects it to risks of retaliatory tariff uncertainties given the precarious nature of U.S.-China trade relations.

In the latest development, Intel’s also been reportedly mulling the cancellation of external volumes of its foundry unit’s 18A process node, with intentions to skip straight to the more advanced 14A technology. The accelerated transition timeline appears to be an aggressive attempt to bolster Intel Foundry’s appeal to the likes of advanced chipmakers like Nvidia (NVDA) while secular AI demand remains strong. Although Intel has yet to provide direct confirmation on the matter, this piece of news shouldn’t be entirely dismissed in the stock’s investment decision-making process. It’s a stark reminder that Intel Foundry’s 18A node has yet to secure material external customer interest, which has been repeatedly acknowledged under new CEO Lip-Bu Tan’s leadership.

While the Intel stock has continued to trade resiliently range-bound in the low-$20 levels, we believe its risk profile has become increasingly skewed to the downside. The mounting list of imminent macro, regulatory, trade policy, and company-specific challenges are poised to compound Intel’s existing plate of execution headwinds in leading its turnaround. Taken together, we remain incrementally cautious heading into Intel’s upcoming earnings update, as investors may be in for a rude awakening with evolving dynamics in the chipmaker’s operating backdrop likely setting the stage for further disappointment.

18A's Limited Resonation with Customers

While Intel Foundry initially started off as a key piece to Intel’s turnaround plan under previous CEO Pat Gelsinger’s IDM 2.0 strategy, it’s rapidly become a burden for the company’s fundamental outlook instead. Intel Foundry’s repeatedly been dogged by negative headwinds in recent years, spanning a potential spin-off and/or buyout from a rival to reports of outright rejection from potential customers due to its inferior performance. While some have been actively denied by the management of associated parties, others have been proven without response. For instance, Broadcom (AVGO) has yet to provide endorsement for Intel Foundry nor formally follow-up on earlier reports that it’s turned down 18A, citing the technology as “not yet viable to move to high-volume production” for its needs.

Despite limited interest from fabless chipmakers, Foundry’s scored some high-profile commitments for custom processors from hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT), nonetheless. Yet the lack of transparency on deal size and progress on this front remains a weak link for brewing doubt over the true traction of Foundry’s external roadmap.

Under Tan’s leadership, the first thing he’s repeatedly acknowledged is that there remains more work to be done to ensure the success of Intel Foundry, given 18A’s limited resonation with existing market demand. In the first order of business, Tan’s committed to spending more time engaging with existing and prospective foundry clients to better understand their needs and build trust:

Tan's vision centers on customer-centricity and engineering excellence. He pledges that Intel will “listen closely” to help address customer and partner needs. Moreover, he stresses the importance of embracing a more software-led design approach and designing purpose-built silicon to enable key workloads for customers. Embracing AI-driven system design, Intel will accelerate development of full-stack solutions and purpose-built silicon for specific outcomes, such as new architectures and critical workflows. This reflects Tan's aim to rebuild trust and strengthen ecosystem partnerships.

Source: Intel 2025 Vision Day Keynote Roundup.

…we need to build trust with foundry customers. We have a lot of important building blocks in place…However, I know from my years at Cadence Design that success in foundry business requires more than process technology manufacturing capabilities alone. It is first and foremost a customer service business, built on foundational principle of trust. And we need to instill customer service mindset across our foundry business. Success in foundry relies on recognizing that each customer use different design tools, methodologies, and styles.

Source: Intel Q1 '25 Earnings Call Transcript.

For now, 18A’s largest customer remains Intel Products itself – specifically the upcoming “Panther Lake” client CPU that begins shipping at year-end. This is consistent with management’s expectations that external 18A SKUs won’t be added to the mix until at least later 2026.

More critically, the lagging external adoption is clear confirmation that Intel Foundry’s investments continue to trail its goals of regaining node leadership. Recall Intel management, under Gelsinger’s leadership, had previously touted 18A as the world’s most advanced process node. Yet key rival TSMC (TSM) had, at the time, denied these claims. As we’d discussed in a previous coverage, TSMC management has clarified their belief that Intel 18A’s competitive advantage in performance and cost considerations over its N3P node may exist for Intel Products only:

Charlie Chan

I am very curious about your comments about the technology leadership, because your competitor knows all customer intel, states that their PPA is ahead of your 2-nanometer, even the cost is lower. So, I want to consult your opinion, why there's a different story and how do we judge...

C. C. Wei

…The comment stays the same, so that they are new is the technology would be very similar or equivalent to TSMC's N3P. We further check again with all the specs or the possible published in technology, transistor technology and everything. My comment stays the same with a big advantage in the technology maturity. Because of, in 2025, when they say that their newest technology will be go on production. For TSMC, that will be the third year with a very high-volume production in the fabs…

Mark Liu

Let me add some color to this. I think C. C. has been very modest. I think he's claimed that N3P is comparable to their 18A. We still affirm our statement. But I would like you to look at a different perspective. And what C. C., what the other side, the claim might be right, but it's only to their own product. And IDN typically their technology for their own product. We have foundry us, we optimize our technology for our customers' product. So, that's a big difference…

Source: TSMC Q4 '23 Earnings Call Transcript.

While the jury was still out at the time on whether 18A or TSMC’s N3 and N2 nodes are better, the question seems to have answered itself, given limited external endorsement for Intel Foundry today. What’s more pressing is that volume production of TSMC’s more competitive N2 node is just right around the corner, adding pressure to Intel Foundry’s roadmap. Specifically, TSMC’s N2 process node goes into production later this year, with confirmed commitments from Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) for its next-generation EPYC Venice server CPUs, and Apple (AAPL) for its iPhone 18’s A20 processor. There’s even been reports that Intel itself might be committed to TSMC’s N2 node for the production of its upcoming Nova Lake client CPU, alongside dependence on its internal 18A capacity.

Implications of an Accelerated 14A Transition

Taken together, the reallocation of resources to ramp up external production capabilities on 14A would be a strategic option for Intel if that’s what customers ultimately want, and what’s needed for its foundry unit to maintain performance parity, if not superiority, against key rival TSMC.

We’ve already seen Intel deploy a similar strategy not too long ago. In one of Gelsinger’s final turnaround efforts made for Intel, he led his team in pulling the plug on 20A in favor of skipping directly to facilitating 18A’s go-to-market. The rationale at the time was that 20A’s primarily designed to facilitate internal production of the “Arrow Lake” client processor, which could be outsourced instead and save the company $500 million in unnecessary capex.

While the potential decision to skip external production on 18A would be a similar strategic decision as scrapping 20A, the fundamental implications would likely be entirely different. Unlike 20A, which was skipped before volume production began, 18A is already ramping up output to support Panther Lake’s go-to-market later this year. As a result, suspending 18A’s external production plans scheduled for 2026 would effectively mean forfeiting anticipated future revenues, which could trigger write-offs that the accelerated transition to 14A likely won’t be sufficient to fully offset.

Based on Intel’s latest 10K filing, the company’s disclosed it uses the cost method in valuing its long-lived assets under GAAP. What this means is that the initial measurement of assets – such as property, plant and equipment, and intangible assets – would be based on the total capitalizable cost of bringing it to operations (e.g., installation and development costs). And the value of its assets would be subject to depreciation on a straight-line basis over their estimated eight-year average useful life – which under GAAP is defined as the timeline over which the asset is determined to generate an economic benefit to the business. The value of its assets is also subject to an annual assessment to ensure the recoverable value (i.e., value of total cash flows the asset can generate over its useful life) remains higher than its cost basis. In the event the recoverable value of the asset falls below its cost basis, then it’d need to be impaired for the difference.

In Intel’s case, the potential decision to bypass 18A external volume production would essentially mean relinquishment of any future related revenues that the company had previously assigned to the project. While the exact asset valuation attributable to Intel’s 18A process node is not separately disclosed on the company’s balance sheet, it’s likely it’ll be subject to impairment in the event that external volume production on the technology is axed altogether in favor of a premature transition to 14A. For context, it’s estimated that Intel has allocated about $95 billion out of its total capex over the past four years in Foundry-related developments, underscoring the scale of investment exposed to risks of impairment in the event of an accelerated transition to 14A.

As a result, the potential node-to-node jump would be a strategic rather than financial-driven decision. The debate is likely focused on the trade-off between continuing the ramp-up of 18A capacity despite tepid external demand or accelerating to 14A in hopes of salvaging some existing chipmaker interest that rival capacity may be struggling to fulfil. The latter may prove to be a prudent decision, since Intel Foundry’s 14A node boasts more competitive performance against key rival TSMC’s existing N3 and upcoming N2 nodes. An accelerated transition to external volumes on 14A might just help Intel Foundry catch the last train in facilitating the production of next-generation advanced processors currently in development by fabless chipmakers like Nvidia and Apple as well.

More importantly, the fast-tracked undertaking could potentially prevent Intel Foundry from shipping into an impending slowdown – a costly mistake that the company’s been caught in time and again. For instance, repeated go-to-market delays for Intel’s Sapphire Rapids server CPU eventually led the chip to shipping into a cyclical slowdown in 2023. This caused Intel to essentially miss out on the bulk of the cloud spend era and lose significant revenue share in the server CPU market to AMD’s EPYC series processors.

A similar shortcoming was observed in Intel’s data center GPU strategy at the onset of generative AI momentum. Its only competitive AI processor was the Gaudi 3, which didn’t go to market until later 2024 – almost two years after the debut of OpenAI’s ChatGPT that triggered global interest in generative AI developments. By then, Nvidia’s already made two generational leaps in advanced AI chips – including the advancement from H100 to H200 on the Hopper architecture, and the subsequent debut of the Blackwell architecture backing B200. Similarly, AMD has also made significant strides, launching both the Instinct MI300 and MI325X series accelerators over the same period. Both of AMD and Nvidia’s products maintained superior performance over Intel’s Gaudi slate. This had eventually led management to acknowledge Gaudi’s underperformance, with a subsequent decision to pull the plug on the development of its successor, Falcon Shores, as they go back to the drawing table on Intel’s data center and AI roadmap.

As a result, the accelerated transition to 14A may even be a prescient strategy to fend imminent macro headwinds. The upcoming kick-off of reciprocal tariffs on July 9 will inevitably upend industry cost structures. Although AI spending on advanced hardware has been resilient due to secular tailwinds, they’re not entirely immune to belt-tightening decisions if the economy enters into a protracted downturn. This would be similar to cloud spend optimization trends that’ve emerged during the Fed’s latest monetary policy tightening cycle amidst runaway inflation in 2022, despite secular tailwinds for the technology at the time. The impending risk is further corroborated by the Fed’s decision to resume rate cuts later this year. An extended monetary easing cycle has typically coincided with an extended downturn, as seen last during the Global Financial Crisis in 2008. Taken together, Intel Foundry’s potential decision to fast-track external production ramp-up on 14A with subsequent go-to-market in 2026 could mark its first decisive step in correcting a series of past missteps, and drive real progress towards its turnaround efforts.

Fundamental and Price Considerations

The potential for an accelerated shift in Intel Foundry’s external production roadmap from 18A to 14A essentially harbingers incremental execution risks, given the pressed development timelines to meet the demands of upcoming advanced chip designs in the industry. This would compound impending tariff and regulatory headwinds, to which Intel remains inherently more sensitive to, given its significant China revenue exposure. Taken together, the overlapping challenges could further skew Intel’s risk profile to the downside.

Although we see partial strategic merit in the potential acceleration of 14A external volume production ahead of looming cyclical headwinds, it’d likely come at the expense of significant adverse implications on Intel’s near-term fundamentals. This would be unfavorable to neither its bottom line nor free cash flow trajectory, indicating potential requirements to further tap into external funding and/or risks of extended delays to capacity expansion plans critical for long-term growth.

Taken together, we believe Intel’s fundamental and valuation outlook is skewed to the downside scenario. In this case, our fundamental forecast projects Intel’s revenue to expand at a five-year CAGR of 7.0%. This growth trajectory will facilitate a return to GAAP income in 2026, with the figure to expand further at a four-year CAGR of 15.0% through 2030. This compares to our previously discussed base case projections that illustrate revenue expansion at a five-year CAGR of 10.3%, alongside a return to GAAP income by 2026 with further expansion at a four-year CAGR of 23.0% through 2030.

Our downside scenario price target for the stock is $16 per share, which is 28% below the stock’s currently traded price ($22.2 on July 3 open) and 16% below our base case price target of $19 per share.

The price is computed under a discounted cash flow analysis based on Intel’s downside scenario fundamental projections discussed above. Key assumptions include a 9.3% WACC, reflecting Intel’s capital structure and overall risk profile. We’ve also applied a 3.5% perpetual growth rate on projected 2029 EBITDA in projecting Intel’s terminal value. This assumption aligns with the expected long-term economic growth across Intel’s core operating markets, and reflects the pace at which its steady-state cash flows – proxied by 2029 EBITDA – can grow sustainably.

Risks to the Downside Scenario

Although we remain confident that Intel’s near-term fundamental outlook remains skewed to the downside as internal operating challenges mount alongside an unfavorable macro and policy backdrop, we acknowledge that its market valuation has already been subject to significant correction in the past several years. More importantly, the stock’s maintained resilience at the low-$20 level through the first half of calendar 2025 potentially suggests the worst has been priced in, with markets remaining optimistic in turnaround-driven upside from here on out.

This is corroborated by the Intel stock’s recently positive response to recent developments pertaining to the upcoming Republican tax and spending bill. Specifically, the bill, which is due for finalization on July 4, currently includes a raise in tax incentives for semiconductor expansion plans based in the U.S. to 35%, compared to the current 25%. Chipmakers looking to break ground on new facilities on U.S. soil by 2026 would be eligible for the benefit, which is stackable on top of previously announced CHIPS and Science Act grants.

And Intel is currently viewed as a primary beneficiary, given it’s already broken ground on the “Ohio One” facility, which includes two fabs – namely, Mod 1 and Mod 2 – in New Albany. However, the delayed start of production from later this year to 2030 poses risks to Intel’s eligibility to the increased tax incentive, which remains to be seen. Meanwhile, Intel Foundry’s Fab 52 and Fab 62 Arizona plants currently under construction are unlikely to benefit from the upcoming tax credit bump, since the new capacity’s set to come online later this year and likely won’t meet the criteria as a “new build” before 2026.

Meanwhile, even under prospects of imminent impairment costs if Intel Foundry proceeds with an accelerated transition timeline from18A to 14A, the stock has also continued to deliver nominal gains. This potentially suggests investors’ optimistic focus on the strategy’s long-term positives rather than the near-term escalation of execution risks ahead, despite the added pressure on Intel’s already fragile fundamentals. As a result, our downside scenario projections for Intel could prove onerous in the event the stock stays resilient enough to see through a successful turnaround in Intel’s operations across both its Products and Foundry units.

Final Thoughts

The potential pivot in Intel Foundry’s external volume roadmap from 18A to 14A could introduce deepening challenges to the company’s already difficult turnaround. While the move could prove strategically necessary, it adds further execution risks to Intel’s already fragile fundamental prospects.

Although the stock has maintained relative resilience this year, the underlying operating backdrop has continued to deteriorate, leaving it little room for missteps. Yet the impending kick-off of tariffs and new regulatory restrictions harbingers risk of unlocking an industry-wide shock. This could emulate the market selloff observed in April when President Trump introduced the preliminary list of reciprocal tariffs, which Intel likely remains comparatively more vulnerable to given its dominant China revenue exposure and overall fundamental fragility.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Is The Invesco Galaxy Bitcoin ETF Lagging Peers?
data.description:
With Bitcoin (BTC-USD) getting increasingly gobbled up by the iShares Bitcoin ETF (IBIT) and Bitcoin Treasury companies like Strategy (MSTR)(STRF), not every US-listed Bitcoin ETF has benefited from the same type of inflows as the market leader. In this article, we'll look at the Invesco Galaxy Bitcoin ETF (BATS:BTCO) relative to peers.

The fund's BTC holdings have fallen year to date while the broader US-based Bitcoin ETF segment has seen year to date inflows of over 21% measured in BTC. Is there a reason why the fund has lagged the rest of the market in AUM growth over the last 6 months? I think yes.

Bitcoin Capital Flows and Provider AUM

As of June 27th, the capital flow story for Bitcoin continues to be incredibly impressive in 2025. As of article submission, the digital asset sector is working on 11 consecutive weeks of positive net flows into investment products. Bitcoin's month to date investment product net flow as of June 27th was $4.6 billion. Year date net flow came in at nearly $15 million through the first half of 2025:
