What each of these funds have in common is that they are the bottom 6 US-listed Bitcoin ETFs in the market in terms of assets under management:

Fund Details BTCO BTCW EZBC BRRR BITB HODL
Issuer Invesco WisdomTree Franklin Templeton Investments Valkyrie Digital Assets LLC Bitwise Investments VanEck
Expense Ratio 0.25% 0.25% 0.19% 0.00% 0.20% 0.00%
AUM $569.35M $169.44M $564.43M $641.00M $4.30B $1.72B

Source: Seeking Alpha

For me, the biggest standout difference between these funds is the expense ratios. At 0.25%, BTCO is on the expensive side relative to the selected funds above. But by itself, that doesn't necessarily explain the lack of interest in BTCO. For instance, BITB appears to be a significant outlier in this table. At a 0.20% expense ratio, that fund is closer to BTCO than a fund like BRRR yet has the largest AUM in our sample by far. In my view, there are two key reasons for this.

First, Bitwise Investments is a crypto-native asset manager. This is not an issuer that touches traditional index ETFs and even has a digital asset fund that offers exposure to tokens like Solana (SOL-USD) and Litecoin (LTC-USD). Thus, I think Bitwise has benefited from crypto trustworthiness to a larger degree than each of the other issuers shows. Also, BITB uses part of its management fee to fund Bitcoin development. The other issuer that has committed to such an action is VanEck - so it's likely not surprising to see each of these two funds with the two largest AUMs of the bottom 6 funds.

Still, this doesn't explain why BTCO has performed so badly against a fund like IBIT. I suspect the original roll out of these funds has been a factor. Consider this screenshot from my Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin ETF (FBTC) from January 2024:

Back in January 2024 when these funds originally launched, BTCO would have had one of the higher post-waiver fees in the market at 0.39%. Even though there was a 0.00% waiver on the first $1 billion in AUM or for 6 months, investors looking for long term solutions likely balked at BTCO due to the fee after waiver. BRRR also had a high post-waiver fee and has a very similar AUM to that of BTCO today.

Fund Performance

Ultimately, what likely matters most for potential BTCO investors is how the fund actually performs compared to the products in the market. So far, there is virtually no difference between these funds based on total return over the last year:

We can see the Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (GBTC) is the obvious laggard of the bunch above. That is entirely due to the fund's egregious 1.5% expense ratio. But short of choosing to minimize third party custodian risk to Coinbase (COIN) through FBTC, there is very little difference between these products.

Closing Thoughts

For investors looking for a simple way to get Bitcoin exposure through the ETF wrapper, BTCO will work perfectly fine. Is it the cheapest fund? No. Is it the biggest by AUM? No. In fact, it's one of the smallest by AUM and that is likely due to some combination of the fund's current 0.25% expense ratio relative to slightly cheaper alternatives today and the fact that the product launched with one of the higher post-waiver fees in the market. The lack of early adoption is perhaps haunting AUM growth today. But, again, purely as a Bitcoin proxy, it'll likely work just as well as the other funds that rely on Coinbase for custody.

data.title: Equity Markets Are Looking Past Risk, But What About The Bond Market?
data.description:
Equity markets have largely looked past risks recently, but what about the bond market? MoneyTalk discusses what’s ahead with Alexandra Gorewicz, VP & Director, Active Fixed Income Portfolio Management, TD Asset Management.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Equity markets have largely looked past a variety of risks out there, but what's happening in the bond space? Joining us now to discuss, Alex Gorewicz, VP and Director for Active Fixed Income Portfolio Management at TD Asset Management.

Great to have you back on the program, Alex.

Alex Gorewicz: Thanks, Greg. Great to be here.

Greg Bonnell: We're at the halfway point of the year. We've entered the second half of the year. We have equity markets at all-time highs, no shortage of risk. The equities don't seem to mind. Do the bonds mind? What's going on bonds?

Alex Gorewicz: Not doing very much. Interest rates have been pretty range-bound of late. And I think there are a lot of forces that are pushing and pulling on where interest rates should go from here. So that's actually led to some surprisingly calm trading days in the bond market in the last couple of weeks.

Greg Bonnell: In the last couple of weeks? So we don't even have to go far back to have geopolitical risk. The war in the Middle East. We've had concerns over the US Big Beautiful Bill, what it could mean for the economy. There's a whole bunch of things that, I guess, in regular times, I would think the bond market would react to. What do you think is going on there that the bond market is seeing through these things?

Alex Gorewicz: I think each one of the factors that you've mentioned that historically could have or should have led to much bigger moves in the bond market, or a clear direction being set in the bond market, can actually be seen from multiple points of view.

So, for example, take immigration. This is a relatively easy one because-- well, there's nothing actually easy about it when you think about the pass-through effects to the job market. But when you take immigration policy on its own, a tighter labor market should mean stickier wages that could potentially push up on inflation, that should push up interest rates.

But then longer term, a smaller labor market generally means shrinking economy in the longer run. So all things being equal, those two factors are pushing and pulling on where interest rates should go from here.

I could do the same thing with the budget bill. For example, what's being currently pushed through the House is a substantially higher deficit than the initial move from the House to the Senate with the bill. And an extra half a billion-- oh, sorry, $500 billion to $600 billion of debt that needs to be factored in over the long term, that should push interest rates higher.

On the flip side, we're seeing a lot of commentary from the Treasury saying that they're going to fund a lot of that with Treasury bills. That doesn't mean an increase in bond supply, and that's weighing lower on interest rates.

So you see how all the different factors, all the different events and developments that you've highlighted, each one of them could be analyzed from different points of view and are having this offset, almost balanced impact on how interest rates are trading right now.

Greg Bonnell: From what we keep hearing from Jerome Powell in terms of patience, and this factor could be this but this factor could be that, slowing economy, maybe higher inflation-- they're on pause because they really don't know what to do in this situation. If you're taking a look-- we'll start breaking down the central banks.

But we'll start with the Fed because that's the one the market cares about the most, for the most part. What do we think is going on there? When do we think they might actually get back off the sidelines? What do they need to see?

Alex Gorewicz: So, I think every investor is going to take a slightly different view of the Fed reaction function. In my opinion, labor will matter most. I think if you have negative surprises-- and we had a negative surprise in ADP today-- but if you have a negative surprise, for example, on non-farm tomorrow, I think it increases the likelihood that the July meeting for the Fed is a live one. In other words, that they could very well cut interest rates, which is not fully priced by the market.

But if we go beyond just the next meeting, right now to the end of the year the bond market has two to three rate cuts priced into the Fed. I don't think that there's a lot of consensus around that number. And what I mean by that is, I think that just falling out of the distribution of how investors view what the Fed is going to do-- which is either they're going to be on hold for the rest of the year, or there are a lot more rate cuts that are coming.

And in terms of how I would put myself in either of those camps, I think it just depends on jobs. So if jobs are weak, if they surprise to the downside, I think you're more likely to see more rate cuts than what the bond market has currently priced.

Greg Bonnell: The other part of the equation for the Fed, and obviously the mandate in Canada too, is inflation. And I think there's been, after April 2, even heading up to April 2, all the threat of tariffs and then the big chart of the tariffs-- people said, inflationary, inflationary. It doesn't appear to be showing up in the numbers. What's going on there?

Alex Gorewicz: Correct. I think up to now you've had this Goldilocks outcome for the Fed. And what do I mean by that is jobs numbers have been more resilient than expected by the bond market, by economists, even probably, by the Fed themselves. And then inflation has been more benign or weaker than expected-- exactly like you've alluded to-- than the Fed has expected, economists had expected, investors had expected. So you've had this very positive outcome where jobs are great and inflation is not a problem.

Now we might be at that apex where it's going to go in bad directions on both fronts. What I mean by that is inflation could actually start to tick up from here, and jobs could actually fall from here, or it gets worse in the labor market.

What does the Fed do in that environment? And this is where, I think, there are different investors that have different points of view on what would happen. I believe the Fed's going to care more about the labor weakening, if it does.

Greg Bonnell: How about in Canada? What's going on here? Because, of course, the Fed has a dual mandate. The Bank of Canada is just supposed to be watching inflation, but they've been cautious and patient as well.

Alex Gorewicz: Correct. And I think in Canada you've seen more of that tariff impact on inflation already come through, versus the US, where it seems businesses and the business community is kicking the can down the road on passing tariffs through to consumers. You've seen that actually start to happen in Canada.

Greg Bonnell: And as a result, Bank of Canada is on pause, right? They're taking a much more cautious wait-and-see approach to see, is this transitory? Will it get worse from here? Will it affect more goods and services?

Alex Gorewicz: On the flip side, we've seen manufacturing employment be really weak for several months now in Canada. That's not a surprise. We know the business community has said there's so much uncertainty around tariffs and our trade negotiations with the US. I think if we get more clarity in the next month, the government, our government, has a self-imposed deadline--

Greg Bonnell: July 21st--

Alex Gorewicz: That's right.

Greg Bonnell: --I think, to try to make a deal.

Alex Gorewicz: I was going to say at the end of this month, roughly. But yes. If we get more clarity, I think there are enough green shoots in the Canadian economy that we can get through this soft patch.

What I worry about is if there is going to be persistent uncertainty for Canada and for the business community, I worry that the weakness we're seeing in manufacturing could spill over to the services sector and just the broader economy-- and that's obviously not going to be a good thing.

Right now, from the bond market's point of view, they think Bank of Canada maybe has one more rate cut in them, or maybe they just stay on pause for the remainder of the year. And again, it depends on how things evolve from here, and how much clarity we get around the nature of our economic relationship with the US going forward.

But if things persist, if the uncertainty persists, I'm in the camp that Bank of Canada should cut a lot more.

Greg Bonnell: Quickly, I'll ask you-- that's where we are and where we might be headed in the next little while. We've got six months ahead of us now for the rest of 2025. What are you watching?

Alex Gorewicz: So aside from the labor market, especially in the US-- although, again, also in Canada as it pertains to the services sector-- aside from labor, what I'm watching is what business leaders are saying. And right now, a lot of the sentiment is weak for the second half of the year. I'd like to see if that negative sentiment persists or if we start seeing a rebound.

Original Post

data.title: DSU Can Be An Opportunistic Strategy During Periods Of Economic Uncertainty
data.description:
The BlackRock Debt Strategies Fund (NYSE:DSU) is a closed-end fund [CEF] designed to provide investors with current income and exposure to a diversified portfolio corporate debt and loans. DSU pays out a forward rate of $1.18/share, yielding 11.15%.

DSU primarily invests in issuances that are rated BBB and lower, making this a high-yield strategy. 53.73% of the portfolio is made up of B-rated bonds; 28.54% of the portfolio’s issuances are BB-rated, and roughly 12% are CCC-rated or below, including not rated.

DSU is well diversified, spreading the risk across a total of 1,250 individual issuances. The weighted average life to maturity for the portfolio is 6.11 years, providing medium-term exposure in the corporate debt market. The average yield to worst across the portfolio is 9.68%, suggesting substantial upside potential for the held issuances.

The portfolio strategy primarily invests in US bonds and loans with little international exposure. 90% of the total weight derives from US issuances, nearly 4% in Canada, 2.34% in Luxemburg, and 1.14% in the UK.

Term loans make up the majority of the weight in DSU, commanding 76.32% of the total portfolio weight. High-yield bonds in the industrials sector is the second largest weighting at 13.72%. Given the growth potential of the industrials sector, particularly with nearshoring & reshoring, I suspect this strategy could be beneficial to DSU investors.

DSU pays out a monthly distribution with an annualized forward rate of $1.18/share, yielding 11.15%. Though the historical payout has varied from year to year and in some instances reduced, DSU has increased its distribution rate multiple times throughout the last 3 years.

Investors should be aware that distributions can be categorized across a variety of streams with the most recent DSU distributions being a return of capital. I recommend investors consult their financial planner prior to making a final investment decision to fully understand the potential tax implications.

DSU charges a relatively appealing fee of 1.24% considering the fees from similar funds. In terms of depth and liquidity, DSU has $536mm in assets under management with 244k shares valued at $2.6mm trading hands on a daily basis, providing adequate liquidity for investors seeking to enter or exit a position.

DSU has performed exceptionally well during long time frames, outperforming the majority of its peers on a 5-year and 10-year basis.

Taking performance into consideration, investors must recognize that high-yield bonds don’t offer the same stability as investment-grade bonds. High-yield bonds oftentimes perform similar to equities given the broader risk implications.

From a high level, the differentiator between high-yield and investment-grade issuances is the credit quality and operational performance of the organization. High-yield debt is typically issued by companies that may not be in the best financial position, potentially exhibiting signs of flagging growth, declining margins, or higher levels of leverage. In general, interest payments may hold a higher weight in terms of profitability for these companies, potentially posing the risk of solvency during times of economic stress and uncertainty.

For example, international trade disruptions, like the one that resulted from the imposed tariffs by President Trump, may result in increased costs to these organizations, potentially impacting the bottom line more severely when compared to investment-grade organizations. July 9, 2025 is the deadline for the tariff pause, bringing forth a wave of appealing trade deals or the return of heightened import duties. Though I suspect many trading partners will come forth with a win-win agreement with the US at the risk of lost business, there may be certain partners that do not come to an agreement, potentially resulting in Liberation Day-style rates.

Uncertainty, when looked through an optimistic lens, can create opportunity for high-yield issuances, as suggested by the sharp value decline in DSU experienced in April 2025. Though the risk of default may be relatively higher as a result of the additional costs from import duties, the portfolio should be diversified to the point of any single default being relatively immaterial to the broader strategy. For this reason, I believe the economic uncertainty has created a substantial opportunity for investors.

Interest rate policy may also impact high-yield bond performance. The general consensus on Wall Street is that the Fed may cut the key rate by 75-100bps in 2025. This could have two impactful implications to high-yield issuances. The first is that the issuances will become more appealing relative to the risk-free rate, potentially driving up the value of these bonds. The second is that the cost to refinance debt may be coming down, potentially positively impacting organizations that may not have the capital or cash flow to pay down maturing debt.

Overall, I believe that this high-yield strategy could perform well given the current economic environment. Though there are many factors at play that could derail the strategy, such as the deterioration of the international trade environment paired with high rates, I do not believe this level of disconnect between President Trump and Fed President Jerome Powell will occur. I am recommending DSU with a BUY rating with a target allocation in the range of 8-12% with the assumption of a mixed portfolio of equities, high-yield & investment-grade bond exposure.

data.title: O-I Glass: A Big Transformation Is Beginning
data.description:
Back in January of this year, one company that I decided to upgrade from a ‘buy’ to a ‘strong buy’ was O-I Glass (NYSE:OI). If you are new to this business, it is worth mentioning that it focuses on the production and sale of glass containers that are used for alcoholic beverages, soft drinks, certain food products, juices, teas, and pharmaceutical products. This is what I have historically referred to as a ‘simple’ business because, operationally speaking, it's not complex at all. It's easy to understand and demand for it will almost certainly always exist.

I made the argument that the plunge that shares had experienced leading up to that point made it very attractively priced. And as such, I claimed that the stock deserved significant upside, especially if we assume that management will come through on their promises for the future. Admittedly, recent financial performance has been slightly negative. But on the whole, I found this enterprise to be too cheap to pass up. Given these factors, I believed then that the stock deserved a ‘strong buy’ rating. And in all honesty, I feel the exact same way now. This is in spite of the fact that the stock has risen 38.5% since that article, easily surpassing the 1.8% rise that the S&P 500 enjoyed.

Shares still offer upside

Recently, the fundamental picture for O-I Glass has been mixed, to slightly negative. Take the most recent data that we have as an example. This would cover the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue was $1.57 billion. That's a hair below the $1.59 billion the business reported one year earlier. This decline occurred in spite of the fact that the company saw revenue in its America's business grow from $854 million to $873 million. That rise, according to management, was thanks to higher selling prices that benefited revenue to the tune of $30 million. Management chalked this up to its ability to pass through higher inflationary costs on to its customers. Total glass container shipments for the region were actually up 4% as well. So clearly, the market was able to absorb these higher prices.

Internationally, the picture was different. In Europe, overall sales contracted from $709 million up to $667 million. Lower average selling prices hit revenue to the tune of $46 million. The good news is that we did see a rise in shipments amounting to 4% year over year. That helped to boost sales to the tune of $18 million. Unfortunately, that was not enough to offset the lower selling prices and the fact that foreign currency fluctuations negatively affected revenue to the tune of $14 million.

On the bottom line, the picture for the company worsened quite a bit. Driven by inflationary pressures, the firm reported a drop in net income from $72 million to negative $16 million. Another big pain point involved $82 million of restructuring, asset impairment, and other similar charges. Most of this involved the firm’s operations in Europe. But there were certain corporate costs and costs that were incurred here at home. As I mentioned in my previous article, management has been working hard on reinventing the business.

This has been part of its Fit to Win program, which is a major initiative aimed at reducing enterprise costs and at optimizing things like the firm's network and value chain. Specifically, this falls under its Horizon 1 push that began in 2024. However, the company does have two other phases of this plan that will begin in the future. Horizon 2 will emphasize leveraging the company's competitive position to drive future profitable growth. And Horizon 3 will see the company grow through geographic expansion, joint ventures, partnerships, and mergers and acquisitions activities. These are expected to begin in 2026 and 2028, respectively. So far, management has incurred $283 million of cumulative charges under the Fit to Win program. And in future quarters, the firm anticipates these costs to grow.

Other profitability metrics for the company have decreased as well. Operating cash flow is the one exception, going from negative $270 million to negative $171. If we adjust for changes in working capital, however, we would get a drop from $216 million to $197 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business contracted from $317 million to $294 million. In the chart above, you can see financial results from 2023 to 2024.

The drop in revenue here was sharp, Driven by not only lower pricing, but by a plunge in sales volumes. Management blamed all of this pain primarily on inventory destocking. In essence, it appears as though customer inventories had grown too much over the previous years, likely in response to supply chain issues that caused a ramping up of inventories. So this is just a return to more normal levels. Naturally, this would cause worsening in profitability also.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year as a whole, management believes that EBITDA will come in at between $1.15 billion and $1.20 billion. A similar year over year growth rate for adjusted operating cash flow would give us a reading of $655 million. But that's not all. As I mentioned already, management has a plan to optimize operations and to create shareholder value. By 2027, the firm expects to generate around $1.45 billion worth of EBITDA. And by 2029, they are targeting $1.65 billion. Assuming that adjusted operating cash flow will rise at the same rate, we would get readings of $808 million and $919 million, respectively.

Another thing to take into consideration here is that management intends to reduce the net leverage ratio for the company to 2.5 by 2027. And long term, they want to keep it between 2 and 2.5. This would mean a reduction in net debt from $4.59 billion today to $3.63 billion in 2027. For 2029, hitting the midpoint of what management is targeting would mean net debt of $3.71 billion. Incorporating this into the equation gives us the ability to figure out how cheap shares are on a forward basis for 2027 and 2029 if management can hit their objectives.

In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using these estimates and using historical results for 2024. These are already incredibly low multiples. In fact, in the table below, you can see how the company stacks up against five similar firms using the 2024 figures. In each case, O-I Glass is cheaper than the competition. And with only one exception, I would describe it as being drastically cheaper.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
O-I Glass 3.9 6.3
Ardagh Metal Packaging SA (AMBP) 4.0 10.3
TriMas (TRS) 15.2 12.6
Myers Industries (MYE) 7.9 9.9
Greif (GEF) 10.2 8.7
Silgan Holdings (SLGN) 9.9 12.3

I think a good argument here could be made for multiple expansion, especially based on management's plans. But even if that does not come to pass, the stock deserves some nice upside. If we assume that the firm does hit its targets for 2027 and 2029, and if we also assume that it continues to trade at the forward multiples estimated for it for 2025, we can see in the chart below what the annualized upside for shareholders would be. In the worst case scenario, we would be looking at upside of about 10.5% per annum. That's roughly what the market averages per year. But in other cases, especially when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, upside could be drastically higher. In this case, we would be looking at upside of 61.6% annually between now and the end of 2027 and 30.1% from now through the end of 2029. I would actually put greater credibility into the EV to EBITDA approach over the price to adjusted operating cash flow approach because of the plan to reduce overall leverage. And honestly, even if we cut these numbers in half, they would still represent market beating potential.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I think that the market continues to underestimate O-I Glass. The company looks incredibly cheap right now and I am of the professional opinion that changes being made by the company should help to instill additional confidence from the investment community into the business. Even if the firm continues to trade at the multiples that it is currently trading for, hitting its objectives for profit growth over the next few years would result in market matching returns at worst and market beating returns at best. This favorable risk to reward situation makes me feel confident in maintaining the firm as a ‘strong buy’ candidate.
